schools.summary: Alcuin/Augustine: he deliberately approaches the place of his Passion; Mary treats him as God, and 'me ye have not always' means his bodily presence.
The 'six days before' idiom (cf. Amos 1:1) dates the supper to Saturday evening; Bethany is the staging-point for the triumphal entry.
Calvin: a lesson in waiting for God's unknown season — those judged rashly who thought Christ would skip the feast (11:56).

outliers.note: The JFB note merged here concerns the Emmaus road and does not bear on v.1.

schools.summary: The same supper as Matthew 26:6 / Mark 14:3 at Simon the leper's; John adds eyewitness details; Lazarus' presence at table is the remarkable note.
Calvin harmonizes — John implies a house other than Lazarus' (he was a fellow-guest); no contradiction with the Synoptics.
Wesley: Martha's standing and the costly ointment suggest a family of some figure who lodged Christ during the last week.
A 'pound' of costly oil of nard; John alone names Mary, true to her place at Christ's feet (Luke 10:39); distinct from Luke's earlier anointing.
Calvin: a compound of many fragrant substances, hence its great cost (the 300 pence of v.5).
John names Judas (where the Synoptics say 'the disciples'/'some'); the standing epithet 'which should betray him' is his habit.
Calvin: by synecdoche the Synoptics spread to many what began with Judas alone, who drew the others in.
Three hundred denarii (~a year's wage); the Synoptics preface it with the charge of 'waste.'
Calvin (citing Pliny): the costliest ointment did fetch ~300 denarii, so Judas's figure is accurate even though his heart is false.
Judas as treasurer of the common fund (cf. 13:29); John's aside unmasks his real motive, unknown to the disciples then.
Clarke: 'bare' is better 'stole' (ebastazen) — the Evangelist shows utter detestation, emphasizing 'he.'
Calvin: the others thoughtlessly condemn Mary, but Judas cloaks covetousness in a plea for the poor — 'what a frightful beast the desire of possessing is.'

debates.question: Does 'bare what was put therein' mean simply carried, or pilfered?

sides.position: carried (as treasurer), with theft implied by 'thief'

schools.summary: Best MSS read 'suffer her to keep it against the day of my burying'; the anointing looks toward his burial (Mt 26:12; Mk 14:8).
Calvin: 'let her alone' rebukes those who needlessly trouble others; the costly act is defended as serving his burial.

outliers.note: The JFB note merged here only remarks on the disciples' earnest discussion.

schools.summary: Chrysostom/Augustine: 'me ye have not always' speaks of his bodily presence — soon to be gone, though in majesty he abides always.
Jesus does not discredit almsgiving but notes the 'relativity of duties'; the poor are a perpetual charge (Deut 15:11), his bodily presence brief.
Calvin: Mary's unrepeatable act is distinct from the daily service due Christ; those who serve him by mere lavish display are 'apes, not imitators.'
The 'common people' come to see both Jesus and the risen Lazarus; the news spread from Jericho pilgrims to a city already asking about him (11:55).
Calvin: God spreads Christ's name through the Lazarus miracle as 'a preparation for stronger faith after his death.'
The Sadducean chief priests, having resolved to kill Jesus (11:53), now plot to destroy Lazarus, whose life refutes their denial of resurrection.
Calvin: 'worse than insane fury' to kill one raised by divine power — the giddiness with which Satan torments the wicked.
Barnes/Clarke: unbelief stops at no crime; Wesley: this is why the earlier Gospels, written in Lazarus' lifetime, omit his story.
The imperfect tenses mark a continuing secession — many 'were going away' from the priests' authority and 'believing on Jesus' because of Lazarus.
Palm Sunday; a great multitude (chiefly Galilean pilgrims); John tells the entry more briefly than the Synoptics.
Chrysostom: the true Passover Lamb enters (cf. Ex 12:3-6); Theophylact: they hailed him as an earthly king to free them from Rome.
Calvin: Christ comes of his own accord to offer himself to a voluntary death; Henry marks the people's respect.
Palm branches from the spot; the cry from Psalm 118:25-26 ('Hosanna... Blessed is he that cometh').
Calvin: the palm is the emblem of victory and royal investiture — they hail Jesus as a new king.
Cyril ties 'King of Israel' to the Davidic oath of an everlasting throne (Ps 132:11).

outliers.note: The JFB note merged here is off-topic and does not bear on v.13.

schools.summary: A 'young ass' (onarion); John mentions it spare­ly only to introduce the prophecy, assuming the fuller Synoptic account.
Calvin: John, the latest Evangelist, notes only the substance, leaving the details to the others.
A shortened quotation of Zechariah 9:9 (John nearer the Hebrew than the LXX); the ass is the mount of peace, not the war-horse.
Wesley: 'Fear not' — his meekness and the end of his coming forbid fear.
A characteristic Johannine note (cf. 2:22; 7:39) — only after Jesus was glorified did the Spirit-taught disciples grasp the fulfilled prophecy.
Calvin: like seed not springing up at once, God's works are not understood immediately; the Apostles fulfil prophecy unawares.
Wesley: God's dispensations are seldom understood at first — faith embraces what it cannot yet grasp.
The eyewitness crowd of the raising (11:45) bore continuing witness; distinct from the festal multitude (a one-letter textual variant on 'that/when').
Calvin: the widely-spread report of the miracle drew the multitude to meet Christ and ascribe to him the Messiah's honour.
Wesley: the Evangelist's restrained style on so grand a miracle is more sublime than any orator's pomp.
Two distinct crowds (followers and greeters) converge; the raising of Lazarus was the one sign that 'carried the entire conviction.'
Wesley: the two streams joined, to go before and follow him, moved by the one miracle.
The Pharisees, watching the crowds sweep by, blame each other for their failed plots and hasten Caiaphas's counsel (11:47, 53).
Clarke: 'the world is gone after him' = the bulk of the people (cf. French 'tout le monde').
Calvin: they goad themselves to greater rage, reproaching their own cowardice as desperate men do.

outliers.note: The JFB note merged here is off-topic and does not bear on v.19.

schools.summary: Real Greeks (Hellenes), not Hellenist Jews — Gentile God-fearers/proselytes of the gate come to worship (cf. Acts 11:20; 17:4).
Wesley: 'a prelude of the Gentile Church' — they came to worship the God of Israel.
Calvin dissents — likely Jews of the dispersion, given Roman laws against conversion.

debates.question: Were the 'Greeks' Gentile God-fearers or dispersion Jews?

schools.summary: The Greeks approach Philip (a Greek name, of Galilean Bethsaida) perhaps as known to them; 'we would see Jesus' = a wish to converse and learn.
Calvin: their seeking access through Philip shows reverence — but is no warrant for praying to departed saints.
Wesley: 'we would see Jesus' — a modest request to a disciple little known to them.
The Greeks reach the two apostles with Greek names (Philip, Andrew); Philip perhaps doubted whether Jesus would receive Gentiles.
Clarke: a model of ministerial union in bringing souls to Christ, against the bigotry bred by self-love.
'The hour' is the predestined hour (2:4; 7:30; 8:20), now come, stirred by the Greeks' request; the words voice his own thoughts.
Clarke/Wesley: 'glorified' through death-and-exaltation, opening the Gospel to all nations — but he must suffer first.
Calvin: 'glorified' refers chiefly to the worldwide publication of the gospel the Greeks foreshadow.

debates.question: Does 'glorified' point chiefly to his death/exaltation or to the worldwide spread of the gospel?

sides.position: the publication of the gospel to the nations

schools.summary: The grain must die to bear fruit — Christ's death, burial, and resurrection; he introduces his death just when they looked for a kingdom.
Calvin: his death is like sowing — seeming destruction that is the cause of far more abundant increase.
Clarke/Wesley: 'I must die to be glorified'; the Lazarus miracle gave the occasion, and wheat the apt similitude.
A repeated axiom of Jesus (Mk 8:35; Mt 10:39; Lk 17:33); 'loveth his life loses it' is a present, ongoing paradox.
Wesley: loving life more than God's will loses it eternally; hating it in comparison of God's will preserves it.
Calvin: we must let God mortify us to bear fruit; the 'hatred' is set against immoderate love of the present life.

outliers.note: The JFB note merged here is off-topic and does not bear on v.25.

schools.summary: The law of sacrifice applied to followers (the Greeks still in view): follow Christ through death; the servant is where the Master is.
Clarke: Christ master to instruct and employ; the servant gains a twofold reward — to be with Christ and honoured by the Father.
Calvin: Christ invites us by his example to submit to death cheerfully; he admits us only on the condition that we follow his path.
Christ's real humanity — his soul genuinely troubled (echoing Ps 6:3; 42:5), shrinking from the darkness (cf. Luke 22:42-44).
Wesley: 'what shall I say?' is labouring for utterance, not wavering choice; 'save me'/'for this cause came I' show the two sides of his mind.
Chrysostom/Augustine: he shows real feeling against those who thought one beyond pain could lightly counsel death; the Father at once honours him.
Calvin: the shrinking is real conflict yet wholly consistent with his fixed submission.

debates.question: Is 'Father, save me from this hour' a real petition or a deliberation overcome?

sides.position: not a wish but a labouring for utterance / the two sides of his mind
a real deprecation of the human soul, at once submitted

schools.summary: An urgent prayer of the spirit (vs the flesh of v.27); the 'name' = God's character; a rare audible voice from heaven answers.
Wesley/Clarke: glorified by entering the hour, glorified again by passing through it — the cross magnifying every divine attribute.
Calvin: Christ prefers God's glory above his own life — the rule of all right desire that all else give way to it.
The crowd variously heard thunder or an angel (perhaps both thunder and voice, cf. Ex 19:16-17); a mark of eyewitness narrative.
Calvin: 'monstrous' dullness — some hear God's plain voice as confused sound, as men still do daily.
Wesley: even the clearest revelation keeps something obscure to exercise faith.
The voice was a sign for the people, not Christ's support (cf. 11:42); the 'not for me but you' is exaggerated contrast (not merely for me).
Clarke: 'for your sakes' likely the newly-come Greeks, firstfruits of the Gentiles, who lacked the Jews' opportunities.
Calvin: as the incarnation, so every blessing Christ received from the Father was bestowed on our account; he needed no voice.

themes: not for me but for you

schools.summary: 'Krisis' — the world's testing in the next days; 'the prince of this world' (Satan, 14:30; 16:11) cast out by the cross, not the final judgment.
Calvin: Christ boasts of victory over fear and death, describing in lofty terms the advantage of his death.
Wesley: Satan, who held the world by sin and death, is now judged and cast out.

outliers.note: The JFB note merged here is off-topic and does not bear on v.31.

themes: prince of this world cast out

schools.summary: 'Lifted up' (as the serpent, 3:14; 8:28) = the cross; the emphatic 'I' against the cast-out prince; 'draw' echoes 6:44 — drawing all kinds of men.
Clarke/Wesley: after death and resurrection he draws both Jews and Gentiles; those who follow the drawing Satan cannot hold.
Calvin: the cross is the chariot by which the lifted-up Christ gathers all men and raises them from earth to heaven.
John's editorial note (sēmainōn, cf. 18:32; 21:19): 'lifted up' (3:14; 8:28) signifies the literal manner of his death — the cross.
'Out of the law' = the Scriptures (Ps 89:36; 110:4; Isa 9:7; Dan 7:14); many held the Messiah would reign forever and not die.
Henry: they drew false notions by overlooking the prophecies of Christ's suffering and death.
Calvin: malice blinds them to pit Messiah's perpetuity against his death; Wesley: 'he first dies, then abideth for ever.'

themes: who is this Son of man

schools.summary: Not argument but appeal — Christ returns to 'the light of the world' (8:12), urging them to use their brief privilege before darkness.
Calvin: a gentle admonition that is also a sharp reproof and threat — the light will soon be taken from those who shut their eyes.
Clarke/Wesley: the light (Christ and his doctrine) is about to disappear; follow it while it remains.
'Believe in the light' = believe in Christ the Messiah; 'sons of light' a Hebrew idiom (Lk 16:8; Eph 5:8); he then withdraws.
Calvin: 'sons of light' are heirs who enjoy it to the end; he hides from the adversaries burning to destroy him.
Wesley: children of light = children of God, wise, holy, happy; Clarke notes the manner of his hiding.
A summary of all Christ's signs 'in their presence'; the imperfect = stubborn, continued refusal to believe.
Calvin: the Evangelist removes the offence — Christ had clear testimonies, but the blind would not behold God's glory in the miracles.
Henry: the prophets foretold and lamented the infidelity of the many who believed not.

outliers.note: The JFB note merged here is off-topic and does not bear on v.37.

schools.summary: Isaiah 53:1 fulfilled; the prophecy foretold but did not 'compel' — 'hina' may be result as well as purpose (Robertson); no coactive necessity (Barnes).
Calvin: the prediction laid no necessity, yet revealed God's secret purpose — it must have happened though unspoken.

debates.question: Does the fulfilment formula make the prophecy the cause of the unbelief?

sides.position: no — it foretells, not compels (result, not coercion)
God's foreknown design, yet without laying necessity

schools.summary: 'Could not' = a certainty of result from self-incurred obstacles (cf. 5:44), not absolution from moral responsibility (Robertson).
Wesley/Clarke: a just judgment — for willful resistance they were at length left to the hardness of their own hearts.
Calvin grants the harshness yet the justice: God determined to leave them blind, in accord with his purpose, the blame their own.

debates.question: In what sense could they 'not' believe?

sides.position: judicial hardening — the consequence of willful rejection
God's determinate purpose to leave them blind, blame still their own

schools.summary: Isaiah 6:10 (quoted thrice in the NT); the preaching is the occasion, the heart's hardness the cause of the blinding.
Calvin: a judicial abandonment — God makes the obstinate worse as the just penalty, withholding grace (Gill: not by positive infusion of evil).
Clarke: refers chiefly to national judgments; God does not blind to prevent repentance — that would slander him.
'Because he saw his glory' — the enthroned JEHOVAH of Isaiah 6:1 was the glory of Christ (cf. 8:56); a strong proof of his deity (Ex 33:20).
Calvin: Isaiah was shown the glory of Christ to witness of his reign; Wesley: it is expressly the glory of Jehovah, the Supreme God.
Even many rulers (Nicodemus, Joseph) believed but would not confess for fear of excommunication (9:22) — asserted against the nation's blindness.
Chrysostom: the love of human praise prevented the rulers — a passion breeding many sins.
Calvin: a sound-minded remnant amid the general madness, yet their fearful silence is no light fault.
'The glory of men more than the glory of God' (cf. 5:44); Barnes: a speculative faith that leads to no self-denial.
Clarke: four obstacles of faith — regard to men, riches, fear of disgrace, love of human praise.
Calvin: they sought only to avoid disgrace; ambition outweighing the fear of God incurs great ignominy before him.

outliers.note: The JFB note merged here is off-topic and does not bear on v.43.

schools.summary: A public summary closing Christ's ministry; believing on him is not on him alone but terminates on the Father who sent him.
Chrysostom: against the fear that held the rulers, Christ cries — why fear to believe, since to believe on me is to believe on God?
Calvin: a vehement cry to steady their faith, with implied reproof of cowardice; Wesley: the Father and Son are one.
'He that beholdeth me beholdeth him that sent me' — a strong proof of equality with God, language no mere man could use (cf. 14:9).
Calvin: Christ sends us to the Father to give the conscience true peace; Gill: a seeing with the eye of the understanding, not the body.
Christ the light come into the world (3:19; 8:12; 9:5; 12:35) that the believer not abide in the darkness of sin and error.
Calvin: he maintains the certainty of faith — he came to deliver from darkness by believing — and accuses of ingratitude those who still walk in dark.
Clarke: the Sun of righteousness disperses the darkness, but profits only those whose eyes are open to receive him.
Present errand of mercy — 'I came not to judge but to save' (3:17); a textual variant reads 'keep them not'; future judgment reserved (9:39).
Calvin: he begins to strike the rebellious yet mitigates it, delaying judgment because he has come for the salvation of all.
To 'reject' (athetō, render void) Christ's word is to be judged by that very word at the last day; conscience and the remembered word condemn (Heb 4:12).
Clarke: men will be tried in the great day on the maxims they heard from Christ's mouth (Mt 25:31ff).
Calvin: a dreadful threat — his doctrine alone suffices to condemn the disobedient, as Moses would accuse them (5:45).
Christ has a permanent commission — the Father gave commandment 'what I should say'; the recurring claim (5:30; 7:16; 8:28; 10:18).
Calvin: he sends us to the Father lest his human appearance lessen God's majesty — his doctrine proceeded from God himself.
Clarke: he taught not for secular interest nor self, but the everlasting truths of God.

outliers.note: The JFB note merged here is off-topic and does not bear on v.49.

schools.summary: The Father's commandment is life everlasting; Christ's words are spirit and life (6:63, 68) because their source is the Father.
Calvin: he applauds the fruit of his doctrine that all may yield; the wicked justly feel God's vengeance for refusing the Author of life.
Clarke/Wesley: the commission to preach salvation; the commandment kept is the way to, and beginning of, everlasting life (1 John 5:11-12).
